B. Procedure 
 
Fig. 7. Flowchart of Printing Process 
Initially, a pad with silver Nano-particle ink is inkjet printed 
on the polyimide film, following the design shown in Fig. 6 
(left). The ink is thermally sintered to evaporate the surfactant. 
The print is brought to DW. After alignment, a silver conductive 
adhesive is printed with the design shown in Fig. 6 (right). The 
components are mounted, and th e whole assembly is cured 
thermally at the recommended conditions of silver ECA. All of 
the SMDs are mounted using a semi-automatic pick-and-place 
machine for proper alignment. A flowchart detailing the 
procedure is shown in Fig. 7. 
III. R
ESULTS AND DISCUSSION 
Results of the process study done on both the printing 
technologies are presented. This section is divided into three 
sub-sections: IJ printing; DW printing; and mounting of SMD 
components. More details on these are provided next. 
A. IJ Printing 
For additive interconnection between a SMD and flexible 
substrate, IJ printing is done first in which trace with SMD 
footprint is printed on the polyimide film. Now, with any 
printing technique, to realize a print, one must optimize the print 
process parameters to obtain a fine quality print. In IJ printing, 
most of the work comes in determining the correct waveform 
parameters for stable jetting of the droplets. Basics of a 
waveform and some of the parameters are discussed in 
Introduction section. In this section, a closer look on those 
parameters and their effect on the jetted droplet is shown. Final 
selected waveform is presented along with the droplet 
formation. Aside from the print parameters, effect of post-print 
parameters such as sintering temperature and time is also 
studied. Electrical and mechanical properties are characterized 
for various temperatures and times. 
 
 
Droplet Formation for Optimized Waveform 
Waveform parameters were optimized for the ink used, and 
the droplet formation was recorded, shown in Fig. 8. This figure 
is recorded from the time the droplet exits the nozzle with a 
ligament that is shown at 50 s up to 200 s where a clean round 
droplet is formed. 
 
Fig. 8. Droplet Formation for Finalized Waveform 
Effect of Voltage on the Droplet 
Voltage parameter in an inkjet waveform can have a great 
impact on the droplet speed and its volume. It determines how 
much to deform the PZT element forcing the ink to move within 
and eject a droplet. It consists of two voltages: ON voltage and 
OFF voltage. When a ON voltage is applied, it deforms the PZT 
to expand the ink channel and draw the ink. The ink channel is 
then contracted by OFF voltag e that pushes the ink out the 
nozzle in a form of droplet. In general, for the print head used, 
ON voltage is set to be twice of OFF voltage. Fig. 9 shows the 
effect of increase in ON voltage on the droplet formation. This 
is recorded at the same moment of time, that shows the droplet 
speed increases with the increase in voltage, up-to a point. It 
shows an optimal voltage value after which satellites or 
ligaments start to form and incr ease. This satellite formation, 
apart from bad print, can also result in circuit shortage. 
 
Fig. 9. Effect of Voltage on Droplet Formation

Effect of Pulse Width on the Droplet 
Pulse width determines how long to deform the PZT 
element for jetting the droplets. When the ON voltage is 
supplied and the ink channel is expanding to draw the ink, the 
ON pulse width comes into play that specifies the time to keep 
supplying the ON voltage. Considering the length of ink channel 
and the time pressure waves take to travel back and forth, it has 
a great impact on the speed of droplet.  
 
Fig. 10. Effect of Pulse Width on Droplet Formation 
 The pulse width consists of ON pulse width and OFF pulse 
width. In general, OFF pulse width is set to be twice of ON pulse 
width. Fig. 10 shows the effect of varying ON pulse widths on 
droplet formation at the same moment of time. Clearly, for a set 
ON voltage, higher droplet speeds are seen at higher ON pulse 
width. But after a certain point, here 8.0s, the movement inside 
the ink channel beco mes out of sync, and speed starts to 
decrease. 
Effect of Phase Width on the Droplet 
 Phase width is another wavefo rm parameter that determines 
the speed consistency of all the droplets. Since the print head 
used is a shear mode type head, only one nozzle jets at a single 
time. The KM512 head uses a 3-cycle drive technology that 
divides the nozzles into groups of 3 for timed ejection control. 
Between each jetting nozzle, there is a 70s delay that results in 
droplet formation shown in Fig. 11. For a phase width chosen to 
be 43s, the speed of the droplets appears to be consistent. 
 
Fig. 11. Droplet Formation for Finalized Phase Width 
Long Term Stability of the Droplet 
 Long term stability is more of a reliability parameter than 
waveform, that shows the stability of the droplet over certain 
number of runs. In Fig. 12, it is clear that the droplet formation 
over 40 number of runs is quite  stable for the final waveform 
selected. In this context, the number of runs implies the number 
of times jetting has stopped and started again. 
 
Fig. 12. Long Run Stability of Droplet 
 
Fig. 13. Droplet Speed over 40 runs 
 
Fig. 14. Droplet Volume over 40 runs 
To quantify the droplet furt her, speed and volume of 
ejected droplet was recorded during the 40 runs, and plotted to 
see the variability. Fig. 13 shows the droplet speed and Fig. 14 
shows the droplet volume when ej ected from the nozzle. Both 
of these parameters are recorded from the printer software, 

however for droplet volume, it was manually calculated as well, 
knowing the pitch of the nozzle. Calculated droplet and volume 
recorded from the software provide a good agreement between 
them. 
Effect of Post-Print Parameters 
Post print parameters in additive printed electronics include 
mostly sintering conditions. Sintering temperature and time can 
have detrimental effects on the electrical and mechanical 
properties of the printed material. Different samples were 
printed and subjected to various temperature and times, which 
were selected based on the r ecommended sintering conditions 
for the ink listed in TABLE II. Fig. 15 shows the graph in which 
sintering time was kept constant for 30 mins, but temperature 
was varied. Very high resistivity was seen initially since the ink 
was not sintered completely, and with increase in temperature, 
it dropped down to a reasonable value. Multiple samples shows 
the repeatability of the test. Fig. 16 shows the graph in which 
sintering time was varied but temperature was kept constant at 
150°C. Lot of variation was seen between samples when 
sintered for 15 mins, but it reduced when the time was increased 
to 30 mins, and then negligible difference was noticed. 
 
Fig. 15. Effect of Sintering Temperature on Electrical Resistance 
 
Fig. 16. Effect of Sintering Time on Electrical Resistance 
 
Fig. 17. Effect of Sintering Temperature on Shear Load 
 Effect of sintering post-print parameters on the mechanical 
property, shear load to failure, was also quantified. Looking 
back at the results shown in Fig. 15 and Fig. 16, sintering time 
was kept constant for 30 mins for the shear load to failure test. 
Multiple samples were tested for repeatability. Fig. 17 shows the 
graph of shear load to failure that was done on different samples 
sintered at 100°C, 150°C, and 200°C. Initial increase the shear 
load is to be expected because of the surfactant evaporation and 
increase in adhesion of silver Nanoparticles to the polyimide 
film. However, there is a decrease in shear load when the sample 
was sintered at 200°C. For this explanation, optical pictures 
were recorded for the samples. For the sample sintered at 200°C 
for 30 mins, it can be seen that the cracks start to develop that 
reduced the adhesion between  the printed material and 
polyimide. Optical pictures at various temperatures along with 
zoomed-in view of the cracks ar e shown in Fig. 18, with a 
sheared line shown in Fig. 19. The shear test was done using 
Dage 2400PC Ball Shear Tester instrument. The shear tip was 
brought to the polyimide surface near the printed trace, and the 
shearing of line was done perpendicularly as shown in Fig. 19.

For the height of the printed design, a White Light 
interferometry scan was performed. It is an optical non-contact 
method that uses a beam-splitter to divide the light source into

1µm, as can be seen in Fig. 23.

RLC measurements are carried out at various frequency

from frequency of 10 kHz to 500 kHz. The instrument used is

C. Statistical Study

in IJ print process. The technique used in this study is control 
chart. Control charting is a pro cess that measures the variance 
in a process over time. It cons ists of user specification limits, 
control limits that are calculate d based on means, standard 
deviation and number of sub-groups. In addition to the limits, it 
also consists of means of the sub-groups as well. Based on the 
number of sub-groups and the size, different types of control 
charts are available. The control limits are calculated using the 
following equations.

IV. SUMMARY AND CONCLUSIONS 
Direct write printing techniques such as IJ and micro-
dispensing plays a prominent role in printed electronics for their 
high throughput allowing for rapid manufacturing of low-end 
electronic devices. However, both of the techniques requires a 
good understanding of the complete process for a fine print 
morphology. In the case of IJ, droplet formation and 
optimization of waveform for the print head used comes to be 
quite significant, while for DW micro-dispensing, timing of the 
material flow can affect the print greatly. In this paper, both of 
the techniques are utilized to achieve additive interconnections 
between a SMD and the substrate. Fi rst, IJ is used to print the 
trace and SMD footprint, followed by dispensing of conductive 
adhesive on just the footprint using DW micro-dispensing, and 
finally mounting of SMD components using semi-automatic 
pick-and-place machine. Substantial part of this paper is 
focused on IJ and process optimization for it. In IJ printing, 
waveform parameters tend to be more important since it affects 
the droplet speed, size, and shape. The first section of this paper 
discussed the effect of various waveform parameters on the 
droplet formation. Parameters taken into consideration are 
voltage, pulse width timing, phase width, and one reliability 
parameter, long term stability. After the finalization of 
waveform parameters, effect of post-print parameters such as 
sintering temperature and time is discussed on the electrical and 
mechanical properties of the printed feature. At lower 
temperatures and times, the pr inted feature was partially 
sintered that resulted in high resistance. There was negligible 
difference in the resistance when the temperature went above 
150°C. Similar observation was made for sintering time. For 
constant temperature of 150°C, there was negligible difference 
between time of 30mins and 45mins. However, in the case of 
shear load to failure, sintering at temperatures above 150°C, 
keeping the time constant, cracks start to develop that degrades 
the print feature and its adhesion to the substrate, that is evident 
in Fig. 18. With the process parameters taken care of, SMD 
components are mounted next. For verification of electrical 
connectivity, a green LED was also mounted and shown. 
Process parameters for dispensing of ECA is not discussed in 
this paper. At the end, a statistical study is done. Control chart 
is plotted that shows the variation in IJ printing of around 500-
600 drops over time. The varia tion stayed under the user 
specified limits that consider s the ±4% variance from the 
defined drop diameter, and the control limits as well that are 
calculated based on the sub-gr oup size, mean, and standard 
deviation. With this study, authors aim to quantify some of the 
process parameters and their ef fect on the droplet formation 
from IJ printing for fine print quality, and to achieve additive 
interconnections between the COTS SMD components and a 
flexible substrate.

Abstract— Printed electronics are rapidly increasing 
and competing against th e traditional way of 
manufacturing electronics. Different aspects of the 
processes are already being taken over that avoids material 
wastage, reduces cost and lead times. For example, printing 
technologies are v ery popular in depo siting reliable and 
very fine conductive traces as opposed to conventional 
etching. Previously, authors have developed process studies 
by utilizing Aerosol Jet Printing technology in realizing 
multi-layer metallization wi th successive metal and 
dielectric layers and additively printing a donut micro-via. 
However, the merits of printing  technologies do not stop 
here. Recently, assembly of Surface Mount Devices (SMD) 
on a flexible substrate has been of interest that requires low 
processing temperatures. Conventionally, this is done by 
components mounted on a so lder paste and reflowing to 
about 220 °C. This temperature is extremely high and 
induces many reliability concerns. With the use of printing 
technologies, this can be avoided. A viable method would be 
to utilize low temperature solder for which the peak reflow 
temperature could be mu ch lower than 220 °C. 
Nevertheless, with new methods, there comes new 
complications. For example, reliability con cerns involving 
the interconnection joints between SMD and substrate. 
Since this is to be done additively, another concern comes 
about the printer’s capability to dispense the solder paste as 
well as the metal conductive pad simultaneously. Having to 
additively print conductive pad, and screen print the solder 
brings about much manual work and just not feasible. In 
this work, authors have utilized state-of-art micro-dispense 
additive printer to dispense  the metal conductive ink and 
the Low Temperature Solder (LTS) for SMD attachment. 
Process studies are done that addresses the physical profile 
of the low temperature solder for various print parameters. 
Multiple SMDs such as resistors, capacitors, and Inductors 
are mounted with semi-automatic pick-and-place machine. 
The reflow will still be done bu t at a lower temperature. 
Process development of additive part of the assembly will 
be done, along with the char acterization of physical, 
electrical and mechanical properties.
  
Keywords— direct writ e, printed electronics, low temperature 
solder, flexible substrate, surface mount devices.

I. INTRODUCTION 
Flexible Hybrid Electronics  (FHE), a field that is 
continuously evolving and expanding its footprint, is already on 
its way to be the next revolution in printed electronics industry. 
A recent report from IDTechEx finds that the global demand 
for FHE will reach a value of over $3 billion by 2030 [1], i.e. a 
huge market potential. The term ‘FHE’ is loosely translated as 
the integration of additive prin ted conductive elements with 
traditional manufactured hardware. The reason for this 
integration is mainly because of the benefits that additive 
technologies provide that can be difficult to achieve with 
conventional processes. The major benefit comes with the 
flexible substrate, usually a polymer that can conform to any 
type of surface. This type of substrate comes out to be quite 
incompatible with the resists and etchants that are used in 
conventional lithography. Another benefit with additive 
processes involves the single-step process in which a single 
CAD file is needed for patterning  as opposed  to multi-step 
etching. These benefits and more combine together to provide 
an excellent alternative for the manufacturing of low-cost 
flexible electronics. Different additive technologies are being 
explored that can match the resolution achieved from 
conventional lithography, and also to be compatible with 
various materials. Some of them include Aerosol Jet, 
DirectWrite, and Inkjet. Each of these can achieve a resolution 
of <10m. But with respect to materials, one technology cannot 
978-1-7281-8539-2/$31.00 ©2021 IEEE                                    972                                                 20th IEEE ITHERM Conference  

incorporate all kinds of material s, such as pastes, inks. For 
example, Aerosol Jet is capable to material viscosity ranging 
from 1-1000cP, Direct Write is capable to dispensing thick 
viscous pastes up to 100,000cP, Inkjet limited to narrow 5-30cP 
materials. Whatever the limita tion, each of these can offer 
something better than the other. 
Much work has been published in the field of printed 
electronics. Although the production has been around for 
decades, the development is s till ongoing that  allows the 
manufacturers and researchers to bypass the conventional 
manufacturing techniques. A detailed review on the state-of-art 
technologies for different direct-write printing of electronics is 
discussed in [1], with materials, processes, and applications for 
flexible printed electronics discussed in [3]-[4]. Process studies 
are also investigated involvin g the conductive inks that are 
used, such as [5] discussing the fabrication of tracks using 
Inkjet printing, [6] characterizing the metal-based Inkjet inks, 
[7] focusing on the comparison of Microparticle and 
Nanoparticle based Ag inks. Demonstrations of the additive 
technologies have been shown in various forms including 
antennas [8]-[10], RFID tags [11], harmonic transceiver [12], 
and passive electronic elements [13]-[15]. Even though many 
electrical devices have been completely additively printed, such 
as thin-film transistors [16], inductors [17], capacitors [18], and 
batteries [19], it is not feasible to replace them all in an 
electronic system because of the yield and performance as 
compared to traditional silicon components. Hence, the rise of 
hybrid printed electronics, that involve combination of printed 
and COTS components. Therefore, it is imperative to study the 
interconnection materials that can be deposited between the 
printed tracks and the compon ent. Conductive epoxies and 
adhesives have been exploited to achieve the interconnection 
using Inkjet technology [20]-[21], flip chip with conductive 
bumps on paper assembly technology [22]. Other than 
conductive epoxies, low temperature solders have been recently 
gaining interest to avoid local thermal damage to heat sensitive 
elements, but not much open literature can be found to address 
the resiliency of this material. Most commonly, low 
temperature solders consists of bismuth (Bi) or indium (In) 
alloys with tin (Sn) and small addition of silver (Ag). Bi tend to 
have higher strength but reduced ductility while, In lower 
strength and higher ductility. In also tends to be expensive than 
Bi. Some of the studies with lo w temperature solders consists 
of characterization of flip chip solder joints [23], 
thermocompression bonding in Ag-In solders for packaging 
applications [24], quantifying the properties of Sn-Ag-Bi 
solders [25]. 
Process development of additive interconnections with low 
temperature solders is an area where literature gap seems to be 
vast and needs to be explored more. Often times, with any 
material, the properties are listed or provided for the best-case 
scenario. In the article, this gap is addressed by process studies 
that greatly impact the mate rial characteristics including 
physical and electrical properties. With these additive 
technologies, there are multiple pa rameters that greatly affect 
the quality of the print. In this paper, DW technology is used to 
realize additive interconnections. First, a metal conductive pad 
is printed with silver ink. Next, a low temperature solder is 
printed on top of the pad for the interconnection with SMD. 
After the SMD is mounted using semi-automatic pick-and-place 
machine, the substrate is subjected to reflow. Since this is a low 
temperature solder, the peak temperature is much lower than the 
standard lead-free solder. 
A. Direct Write Micro-Dispensing Technology 
Micro-dispensing or direct-write is another digital printing 
technique that does a remarkable job in additively printing wide 
range of materials. It does so in exceptionally small volumes 
with very high precision, that is achieved because of its ability 
to dispense much closer to the substrate surface. Micro-
dispensing is loosely translated as dispensing of material using 
a certain amount of pressure and pressing it out through a small 
orifice onto a substrate. To ge t into more details about the 
process and the technology, the discussion will be focused on 
one of the products from nScrypt, Inc, who have become the 
pioneers in this technology. The work in this paper is done on 
a 3Dn Tabletop printer that cons ists of a quad head assembly 
with SmartPump
TM and nFDTM gizmos. The system also comes 
with a process view camera and laser for alignment purposes, if 
necessary. Since the materials used are conductible metal 
pastes, and considering that the nFDTM is filament fed material 
extrusion tool, more details on the SmartPumpTM gizmo and its 
working are discussed next. 
The SmartPumpTM gizmo mainly consists of a valve rod, 
the most important component in the gizmo housing that 
controls the flow of material extrusion. It requires the material 
to be loaded into a syringe, with one end connected to the 
pressure tube and the other to the valve body. The “open” and 
“close” position of the valve rod defines when to let the material 
flow and when to stop it without any kind of leaking or 
drooling. This movement of valve rod happens automatically, 
once the positions are defined, in a coordinated manner as per 
the requirement of design file. These positions along with few 
other process parameters can have a drastic impact on the 
quality of the print. Some of them include print speed, material 
pressure, nozzle height, that need to be set as well. The benefit 
of not having a complicated setup with the material being well-
contained in a syringe makes it a perfect additive tool for 
advancement of the manufacturin g of 3D printed electronics. 
More details on this and other gizmos can be found on the 
company website [26].

II. EXPERIMENTAL 
One of the advantages of the additive printing technology 
is the ability to deposit any type of pattern, if provided with a 
simple CAD file, keeping the resolution of printer in mind. The 
test design for this paper was modeled in such a way that the 
interconnection between SMD device and flexible substrate is 
achieved. The modeling was done in AutoCAD software for the 
generation of appropriate DXF file needed for DW printing. In 
the design, common SMD discrete such as resistors, inductors 
are included. Additionally, a LED is also mounted for 
verification of electrical property. The design is shown in Fig. 
2. 
  
Fig. 2. Test Design for SMD Interconnection 
A. Material Properties 
The DW printing technology us ed in the paper utilizes a 
thick viscous paste as its depositing material. Therefore, a silver 
metal conductive paste is used to deposit the trace with SMD 
footprint and a low temperature solder paste is used to attach the 
SMDs. Low temperature solder was selected because of its low 
temperature processing and the solderable ability of the silver 
paste. The low temperature solder is also additively printed that 
allows the whole manufacturing process to be additive. Typical 
properties of both the material s are listed in TABLE I and 
TABLE II.

B. Procedure 
Considering the complete process is to be additive, the 
process control of achieving the interconnection is imperative. 
Initially, a silver metal conductive paste is used to deposit the 
trace and pad geometry. The past e is thermally sintered to 
evaporate any surfact ant using the recommended conditions. 
The design is brought over again, and after aligning, the low 
temperature solder is deposited on the SMD footprints. The 
components are mounted using a semi-automatic pick-and-place 
instrument. After the mounting of SMDs, the assembly was 
subjected to a reflow profile, shown in Fig. 3.  The flowchart 
depicting the process is shown in Fig. 4.

A. DW Printing 
Direct write or micro dispensing technology is used for 
depositing silver tracks and low temperature solder material. As 
discussed in Introduction section, with direct write, the open and 
close valve rod position is the most important parameter that 
controls the flow of material. Other parameters such as pressure, 
print speed, and layer height are also influential to some extent. 
The effect of these parameters need to be studied in detail to 
optimize the process flow for SMD interconnection. The process 
parameters for dispensing of the silver metal paste will not be 
discussed in this paper, however the surface profile for the 
printed material is shown. For the low temperature solder, the 
discussion is in the following section. 
Silver Metal Ink

pads using DW. The process of optimizing the print parameters 
are discussed in another paper. The surface profile is shown in

Electrical resistivity of the additively deposited low 
temperature solder was also qu antified and compared with the 
properties published in literature . To measure the resistivity, 
silver metal line is printed as a base layer, on top of which 
solder was deposited. Measured resistance was subtracted from 
the resistance of metal base layer, which was used for resistivity 
calculation. Fig. 13 shows the variation in electrical resistivity 
of the solder for about eight samples. The average resistivity of 
57Bi-42Sn-1Ag was found to be about 3 times the resistivity of 
58Bi-42Sn. Resistivity of 58Bi-42Sn was obtained from 
published literature [27]. 
 
Fig. 13. Electrical Resistivity of Low Temperature Solder 
B. SMD Discrete Attachment 
Surface mount discretes are mounted via complete additive 
process. A solderable silver in k is dispensed initially for the 
SMD footprint and trace pad, followed by low temperature 
solder on the SMD footprint. The discretes are mounted using 
a semi-automatic pick-and-p lace machine, and the whole 
assembly is put through reflow. With the low temperature 
solder, the peak reflow temperature is much lower (160-165°C) 
as compared to standard lead  free SAC solders (240-250°C), 
that can be beneficial if the components cannot sustain high 
temperatures. Both the material s are dispensed using the DW 
printing technology. Fig. 14 (left) shows the SMDs resistors 
and inductors mounted on the flexible substrate. A LED is also 
mounted for display of electrical connectivity as well, shown in 
Fig. 14 (right). 
 
 
Fig. 14. SMDs on Direct Write Deposited Tracks and Low Temperature 
Solder 
Repeatability in mounted SMD resistance is also 
quantified. Sample to sample variation was found to be 
negligible relative to the rated resistance of 1.5 kΩ, as shown in 
Fig. 15. 
 
Fig. 15. Sample to Sample Variation of SMD Resistor 
Multiple types of SMD discrete components are attached 
including resistors, capacitors, and inductors. The property of 
each of the component was measured at various frequency 
levels to look at the variation. Fig. 17-Fig. 19 shows the effect 
of frequency of resistance , inductance, and capacitance 
respectively, relative to the rated values. These measurements 
are done using an Agilent 4192A LF Impedance Analyzer. Not 
much variation is seen for indu ctor and capacitor at various 
levels of frequency, however, for resistor, there is a huge drop 
in resistance at 500 kHz. This drop is due to the parasitic 
capacitance effect that SMD resistor exhibits at higher 
frequency levels. At lower frequency levels, this effect is not as 
dominant and resistor acts as an ideal resistor.

IV. SUMMARY AND CONCLUSIONS 
Direct write micro-dispensing technology is one of the few 
technologies that continue to prove its efficiency with fine print 
quality and features. Moreover, its ability to dispense wide 
range of materials makes it a very useful gadget for printed 
electronics. However, it does require control of few parameters. 
The most important comes to be the timing of material flow, 
specifically, open and closed position of valve rod that dictates 
when to allow the material to flow and when to stop. Since this 
valve rod position cannot be varied, other parameters that can 
have a significant impact on the print quality are studied. Some 
of them include material pressure, layer height, and print speed.  
To achieve additive interconnection between a SMD and 
flexible substrate, two material s are used: a solderable metal 
conductive ink, and low temperature solder, for which the peak 
reflow temperature was much lower (160°C) than standard 
SAC solders. Process parameters were only varied for the low 
temperature solder, and effect of  those on the surface profile 
was presented. A point to note is that the surface profiles were

studied before the solder was subjected to reflow to look at the 
printed features. Since the print is done on polyimide substrate, 
which is extremely non-wettable and hydrophobic, the solder 
cross section is studied afte r the reflow. The solder on 
polyimide does not wet the substrate and after the reflow turns 
into bulk solder with flux around it. Other than polyimide, 
solder is also dispensed on silver, and copper film as well. With 
silver and copper film on the bottom, solder wets the surface. 
Although for copper, not as much. There is intermetallics 
formation as well. To show the capability of direct write micro 
dispensing printing technique, finally, SMDs are mounted. A 
green LED is also mounted for the verification of electrical 
connectivity. Additionally, a daisy-chain BGA component was 
also mounted on the flexible polyimide to show the potential of 
additive technology.  Quantifica tion of some of the process 
parameters has been presented to dispense a novel low 
temperature solder material for additive interconnections 
between SMD and the substrate. Also, to show the capability of 
DW technology by mounting SMDs, including a BGA package.

On the bending effects o f a printed 4x4 antenna array
John E. Rogers
Boeing Research and Technology 
Huntsville, AL, USA
Abstract—The bending effects of proximity coupled 4x4 patch 
arrays with printed patch elements operating in the X-band near 
10 GHz is presented. The patch arrays have circular patch  
elements with inclusive slots residing on the top surface of four 
layers (10 mil / layer) of DuPont Pyralux AP. The inclusive slots 
decrease the axial ratio of the antenna and increase the impedance 
bandwidth. Each array has a microstrip feed network of quarter- 
wave transformers and T-junction power dividers in the middle of 
the four layer stackup. The microstrip lines are proximity (or  
capacitively) coupled to the patch elements. A ground plane on the 
bottom surface of the four layer stackup provides surface agnostic 
behavior. The patch elements and microstrip feed network are 
printed with DuPont CB028 conductive ink using a liquid dispense 
technique. For the purpose of comparison to PCB processed  
antennas, 4x4 patch arrays with !4 oz. electroplated copper for the 
patch elements and microstrip feed network are also reported. The 
gain, 3 dB beamwidth, and VSWR bandwidth behavior have been 
studied for uniaxial bending of the two arrays for radii of  
curvature down to a 1 inch. The nominal (i.e., no bending)  
measured far-field gain of the arrays is 14.5 dBi (printed) and 14.7 
dBi (copper). The printed and copper arrays behave very similarly 
with changes in gain of 9.3 dB (printed) and 8.3 dB (copper) for 
bending down to 1 inch in an axial direction and changes in gain 
of 2.6 dB (printed) and 2.8 dB (copper) for bending down to 1 inch 
in a circumferential direction.
Keywords—additive manufacturing, antenna bending,
conformal antenna, patch antenna, printed antenna.
I. I nt r oduct i on
Additive manufacturing of 3D printed parts is becoming 
increasingly popular in the aerospace industry for replacement 
of flyaway parts on aircraft. 3D printed parts have low cost, 
weight, and manufacturing time, while still providing high 
structural integrity. Similarly, flexible hybrid electronics (FHE) 
realized by additive manufacturing can be utilized by the 
aerospace industry for areas such as real time structural health 
monitoring, diagnostic testing on the factory floor, and 
improving the in-cabin passenger experience. Printed 
electronics inks can be dispensed by contact (e.g., liquid 
dispense, screen print) and non-contact (e.g., aerosol jet 
dispense, ink-jet dispense) printing techniques.

Subtractive methods, however, have high material waste and are 
time consuming, resulting in high manufacturing cost [1]. 
Additive printing methods based on dispensing printing 
techniques, on the other hand, have negligible material waste 
and have fast printing speeds, resulting in  low manufacturing 
cost [1].
There have been a number of printed antennas [2]-[10] 
reported in recent years. Printed conductive inks are 
advantageous for conformal antennas as they have higher 
flexibility than bulk copper and can be deposited using a number 
of available printing tools; on the other hand, they have higher 
electrical resistivity than bulk copper. For example, the electrical 
resistivity of DuPont CB028, a silver-based conductive ink, has 
been reported as 220 nQ.m [11], which is 14x higher than bulk 
silver. The surface (or sheet) resistance o f a patch antenna is 
proportional to the electrical resistivity or Rs « p . The loss 
resistance of a patch antenna is proportional to the surface 
resistance or RL « Rs.  The far-field antenna gain of a patch 
antenna is related to the loss resistance as

where RRad is the radiation resistance o f the patch antenna.
The antenna gain of a printed patch antenna is limited by the 
electrical resistivity of the conductive ink; however, the decrease 
in gain can be offset by increasing the film thickness.
A microstrip feed network is commonly used for feeding 
patch antenna arrays. A patch antenna fed by a microstrip line 
can have one of three feed types: inset feed, proximity coupled 
feed, or aperture coupled feed [12]. The proximity coupled patch 
antenna typically has higher impedance bandwidth than the inset 
feed as the impedance of the antenna can be matched by 
adjusting both the length of the feeding stub and the patch width- 
to-line width ratio [12]. Pozar reported the first proximity 
coupled fed patch antenna [13] and afterwards reported an 
impedance bandwidth of 13% [14]. A  number of methods have 
been used to improve the performance o f coupled patch antennas 
[15]-[22].
There have been several experimental [23]-[26] and 
analytical [27]-[30] studies on the bending behavior of planar 
arrays as well as adaptive approaches for beam compensation 
[31], [32]; however, to the authors knowledge there has not been 
an adequate study on the bending behavior o f proximity coupled 
planar arrays. Furthermore, there has not been a study of the 
performance of additively manufactured printed in comparison 
to conventional PCB processed copper planar arrays.

In this paper, an array consisting of proximity coupled patch 
antenna elements with inclusive slots above a microstrip feed 
network and a lower ground plane is described. The inclusive 
slots decrease the axial ratio for circular polarization and 
improve the impedance bandwidth [33]. The bending effects of 
printed 4x4 antenna arrays with DuPont CB028 conductive ink 
antenna elements and copper 4x4 antenna arrays with oz. 
electroplated copper w ill be evaluated in the X-band at 10 GHz. 
The antenna arrays are uniaxially bent in axial (axis along length 
of microstrip feed line length) and circumferential (axis 
perpendicular to microstrip feed line length) directions to radii 
of curvature down to 1  inch to determine the bending effects on 
the far-field antenna gain, half-power beamwidth, and 2:1 
VSWR bandwidth. The following sections describe the design, 
fabrication, and experimental characterization of printed and 
copper 4x4 antenna arrays.
II. A nt enna  Desig n
In the earlier years of development of patch antennas, 
transmission line and cavity models were developed for their 
analysis [12]. The transmission line model is a distributed 
element model with an input impedance proportional to the 
patch width or Zin « tanfiW  [12]. The cavity model, on the 
other hand, is a lumped element model (LEM) that assumes the 
antenna behaves like a narrow-band resonator with an input 
impedance equivalent to a parallel RLC circuit [33]. The cavity 
model places boundary conditions on the patch antenna such that 
E-fields are only allowed to propagate in the z-direction or z x 
[Er + E'] = Zs[z x / s] [34]. The cavity model is a simplistic 
and accurate model to describe the patch antenna and w ill 
therefore be used to analyze the 4x4 antenna arrays.
The electrical quality factor Q o f the antenna element is 
related to the radiation quality factor Qr, surface wave quality 
factor Qsw, dielectric quality factor Qd, and conductor quality 
factor Qc as Q-1 = Q r-1 + Q sw -1 + Q d-1 + Q c-1 [36]. The 
losses associated with surface waves, dielectric, and conductor 
are generally small in comparison to the radiation loss such that 
Q ~  Qr.  The electrical quality factor of the circular antenna 
element is derived as [37]

which is related to the VSWR bandwidth, is inversely 
proportional to the quality factor as Af/f0  =  1 / Q . Planar patch 
antennas are inherently narrow-band (i.e., high Q factor) due to 
their small size (i.e., thickness) as predicted by Chu [39] and 
later validated experimentally by Sievenpiper et al. [40].
B. Antenna Elements and Feeding
Ansys HFSS, a finite element method (FEM) numerical 
solver, is used to develop a model of the 4x4 array. The 4x4 
array with its various layers is illustrated in Fig. 1 . The array has 
overall dimensions of 80.6 mm x 55.8 mm. The array is 
composed of circular antenna elements on the surface of four 
layers (10 mil / layer) of DuPont Pyralux AP, a flexible 
polyimide, with £r = 3.4 and tan S = 0.002. A  60 GHz patch 
antenna [41] has previously been reported with DuPont Pyralux
Authorized licensed use limited to: Raytheon Technologies. Downloaded on June 08,2021 at 14:14:43 UTC from IEEE Xplore.  Restrictions apply. 
Fig. 2. Measured electrical resistivity o f DuPont CB028 conductive ink.
TK, which is similar to DuPont Pyralux AP, but with lower 
moisture absorption. The array herein is designed to electrically 
resonate near 10 GHz. The FEM model for a single circular 
patch antenna element with an antenna radius o f 4.15 mm 
predicts a resonant frequency of 10.5 GHz. The addition of a 
quarter-wave slot with a length of 4 mm and width of 0.5 mm 
decreases the predicted resonance of the antenna element to 10.3 
GHz. The width of the slot is numerically determined such as to 
maximize coupling between the antenna element and the 
microstrip feed network.
The microstrip feed network has a four-way power divider 
with two stages: stage one is a single quarter-wave transformer 
[42] fed into a T-junction power divider [43] and stage two is 
two duplicate quarter-wave transformers fed into two duplicate 
T-junction power dividers. Each quarter-wave transformer is 
designed such as to match a 50Q input into a 25Q output using 
a quarter-wave line with an impedance of 35.3 Q. Each T- 
junction power divider is designed to match a 25Q input into 
two 50Q outputs. The insertion loss at 10 GHz through a four­
way power divider used for the 4x4 array is 6.6 dB and the 2:1 
VSWR bandwidth is 6.6 GHz.
The center to center spacing of antenna elements along the 
length of the microstrip feed network is 14.45 mm and the center 
to center spacing o f antenna elements perpendicular to the 
microstrip feed network is 12.45 mm.

III. A
nt enna  Fabr i cat i o n
The copper and printed 4x4 arrays are fabricated using both 
conventional subtractive and additive printing methods. The 
processes for fabricating the arrays are described in the 
following sections.
A. Conductive Ink Evaluation
There electrical resistivity of conductive inks are known to 
be strongly dependent on their curing conditions including: 
temperature, air flow, humidity, and time. In this study DuPont 
CB028 conductive ink was dispensed on DuPont Pyralux AP 
using an nScrypt 3Dn-300 printer. Cloverleaf structures were 
selected to characterize the electrical resistivity of the 
conductive ink. The van der Pauw method is used to determine 
the electrical resistivity of the ink by first applying a voltage 
across two adjacent electrodes then measuring the current 
through two o pposite electrodes as shown in Fig. 2. The 
electrical resistivity is determined by the measured resistance 
and thickness profile of the ink.
The electrical resistivity of DuPont CB028 as a function of 
curing temperature for 30 min cure times is shown in Fig. 2. The 
electrical resistivity of DuPont CB028 cured at 160°C for 30 min 
is 110 nQ.m, which is 6x higher than bulk silver. The results are 
2x better than those reported in literature [11]. The use of liquid 
dispense printing allows silver flakes to be dispensed on the 
substrate more readily than screen printing where the mesh tends 
to block silver flakes. The electrical resistivity of DuPont CB028 
is constant above 160°C due to complete removal of the binders 
and solvents.

B. Fabrication of Copper and Printed Antenna Arrays
The copper and printed 4x4 arrays have four layers of 10 mil 
DuPont Pyralux AP with antenna elements, a microstrip feed 
network, and a ground plane. The conductive portions of the 
copper 4x4 array are constructed in oz. electroplated copper. 
The four layers o f DuPont Pyralux AP are processed to laser etch 
away the unwanted portions of copper using a LPKF ProtoLaser 
U3 355 nm laser machine. The conductive portions of the 
printed 4x4 array are constructed by dispensing DuPont CB028 
in the desired areas using an nScrypt 3Dn-300 printer. The 
conductive ink is then baked at 160°C for 30 min in a Thermo 
Scientific convection oven. The thickness of the antenna 
elements was measured as 30 |am  using a profilometer. The four 
layers of DuPont Pyralux AP for each array are then aligned for 
lamination with three layers o f 3M 966 adhesive tape placed 
between the four layers. The adhesive tape has a 2.3 mil 
thickness with er = 2.9 and tan S = 0.02 that are accounted for 
in the FEM model. Finally, the arrays are laminated with a T- 
Tech multilayer quick press with 48 psi at 70°C for 30 s. The 
fabricated antenna arrays are shown in Fig. 3.
IV. E
xper i ment al  Ch a r a c t e r iza t io n
The following sections w ill address test setup and 
measurement results for return loss and far-field gain 
measurements of the copper and printed 4x4 arrays followed by 
comparing the measured results to those provided in literature.
A. Return Loss Measurement
The copper and printed 4x4 arrays are connected to a high 
frequency network analyzer to evaluate the return loss 
characteristics due to uniaxial bending in the axial and 
circumferential directions. As mentioned ea rlier, the axial
bending direction is the axis along length of microstrip feed line 
length, whereas the circumferential bending direction is the axis 
perpendicular to microstrip feed line length.
The nominal (i.e., no bending) 1.8:1 VSWR bandwidths of 
the copper and printed 4x4 arrays are 2.54 GHz and 2.26 GHz, 
respectively. The VSWR bandwidths of the two arrays do not 
show any significant change for uniaxial bending down to a 1 
inch radius in either the axial or circumferential direction as 
shown in Fig. 4.
B. Far-Field Gain Measurement
The copper and printed 4x4 arrays are placed in the far-field 
to determine their radiation patterns under nominal and bending 
conditions. The Fraunhofer distance, or minimum distance 
needed to be in the far-field, for a 20 dBi horn antenna with a 
width of 3.9 inches operating at 10 GHz is 0.65 m. The distance 
between transmit and receive antennas in the test setup is 4.2 m.
The transmit portion of the test setup for far-field 
measurement includes a synthesized sweeper and a 20 dBi horn 
antenna for transmitting a plane wave across free-space at 10 
GHz. The receive portion of the test setup includes the antenna 
under test (i.e., copper or printed 4x4 array), a 30 dB 
preamplifier, and a power meter with a high frequency power 
sensor. The setup described can be used to determine a 
normalized radiation pattern of the antennas. A known reference 
20 dBi horn antenna, however, is used in place o f the antenna 
under test to determine the actual gain of the arrays.
The nominal (i.e., no bending) antenna gains for the copper 
and printed 4x4 arrays are measured as 14.7 dBi and 14.5 dBi, 
respectively. The FEM model predicted nominal antenna gains 
o f 15.5 dBi (copper) and 15.2 dBi (printed). The measured 
antenna gains o f the copper and printed 4x4 arrays are 0.7 dB 
lower than the FEM m odel. The difference betw een the 
measured results and FEM model is likely due to factors 
including: 0.2 dB o f connector loss, measurement uncertainty in 
the test setup, and possible misalignment between layers. 
Despite the slight differences between the measured results and 
the FEM model, there appears to be good agreement the 
measured results and the FEM model, which uses a measured 
ink thickness of 30 |am  and resistivity of 220 nQ.m for the 
printed antenna elements and printed microstrip feed network.
The copper and printed 4x4 arrays are scanned to determine 
radiation patterns for nominal and bending in the axial and 
circumferential directions. It was determined in the FEM model 
that the main beam o f the antenna array is predicted to be 20 deg 
off boresight (as shown in Fig. 5) due to an intrinsic phase 
difference between antenna elements along the length of the 
microstrip line as a result of serial feeding.
The copper and printed 4x4 arrays had their antenna gains 
decrease by 8.3 dB and 9.3 dB, respectively, for a 1  inch bend 
in the axial direction. The 1  dB difference in gain between the 
two arrays is likely due to misalignment error in the 
measurement setup. The copper and printed 4x4 arrays had their 
antenna gains decrease by 2.8 dB and 2.6 dB, respectively, for a 
1  inch bend in the circumferential direction. The large change in 
antenna gain in the axial direction is predicted by the FEM 
model. The cause o f this change in antenna gain for bending in 
the axial direction is an element to element spacing change of

The radiation efficiency of an antenna is a measure of how 
much power that goes into an antenna is radiated out of the 
antenna. The nominal radiation efficiencies of the copper and 
printed arrays were predicted as 91% and 83%, respectively. It 
was determined through modeling that the radiation efficiencies 
for the arrays did not change as a function of bending down to a 
1  inch bend. Instead, the measured and predicted results show 
that the radiated power is distributed by the 3  dB (half-power) 
beamwidth, which is related to the gain as G <x1 / d 3dB [45].
The 3  dB beamwidth is experimentally determined from the 
data acquired from the radiation patterns. The measured 3 dB 
beamwidth for the copper and printed 4x4 arrays for bending 
down to 1  inch in the circumferential direction is 23 deg, which 
is a result o f the small change in antenna gain. The 3 dB 
beamwidth o f the arrays is 23 deg for antenna bending down to

arrays in comparison to 4x4 serially fed patch antenna [26].
2 inches in the axial direction; however, the 3  dB beamwidth 
significantly increases to 104 deg (copper) and 136 deg (printed) 
for a 1  inch bend, which is a result of a large change in antenna 
gain. It can be determined that for a serially fed array of 
proximity coupled patch antennas the antenna gain and 3  dB 
beamwidth are largely a function of the bending radius for 
bending in the axial direction. The return loss appears to be fairly 
constant for bending in either axial or circumferential directions.
C . Comparison to Literature
As mentioned earlier, there have been have been several 
experimental [23]—[26] studies on the bending behavior of 
planar arrays. Semkin et al. [26] studied the bending behavior of 
a 4x4 serially fed patch array operating near 59 GHz, which is 
comparable to the 4x4 aperture coupled patch arrays discussed 
in this paper. Semkin et al. measured the antenna gain o f the 4x4 
serially fed patch antenna as it was bent in a circumferential 
direction down to a 6 mm radius. For comparison, the copper 
and printed 4x4 arrays described in this paper are also bent in a 
circumferential direction. The normalized gain of the 4x4 
serially fed patch antenna and the copper and printed 4x4 arrays 
as a function of being radius is shown in Fig. 6. The measured 
gain of the arrays described in this paper match well with the 
measured gain of the array from Semkin et al. The measured 
gain of the arrays are within 1  dB down to a 1  inch bend.
C
oncl usi ons
The bending of aperture coupled 4x4 antenna arrays has 
been performed in both axial and circumferential directions to 
better understand gain, 3 dB beamwidth, and VSWR bandwidth 
behavior. The nominal antenna gains in the X-band at 10 GHz 
for copper and printed 4x4 arrays are 14.7 dBi and 14.5 dBi, 
respectively, which match well. The antenna gain for the copper 
and printed 4x4 arrays decreased 8.3 dB and 9.3 dB, 
respectively, for a 1  inch bend in the axial direction. The antenna 
gain for the arrays decreased 2.8 dB (copper) and 2.6 dB 
(printed) for a 1  inch bend in the circumferential direction. The

due to a 4.6 deg chance in phase between elements along the

Comparison of Component Assembly Process-Reliability Relationships of Low-
Temperature Solders and ECAs on Flexible Direct-Write Additive Circuits 
Pradeep Lall, Jinesh Narangaparambil 
Auburn University 
NSF-CAVE3 Electronics Research Center 
Department of Mechanical Engineering 
Auburn, AL 36849, USA 
E-mail: lall@auburn.edu
Tele: +1(334)844-3424
Scott Miller 
NextFlex Manufacturing Institute 
San Jose, CA 95131 
ABSTRACT 
Fabrication of additively printed flexible hybrid circuits 
requires the development of component attachment methods 
with low-temperature processing.  Some substrate materials, 
including thermally stabilized PET and PEN require a peak 
processing temperature of less than 150C.  Several new low-
temperature solder materials have emerged capable of being 
processed at temperatures in the range of 130-150 C to 
access the benefits of lower warpage, lower energy 
consumption, and lower carbon footprint.  This paper studies 
the process-performance-reliability relationships for Sn-Bi-
Ag and Sn-In solders on additively printed copper 
metallization.  Process recipes have been developed for direct 
write additive printers to fabricate single-layer and flexible 
multilayer circuits.  Copper ink is a good and cost-effective 
alternative to silver ink, but its use has lagged due to an 
increased propensity for oxidation during manufacturing.  In 
this paper, photonic curing has been used to sinter copper ink 
to make the traces conductive.  The method flashes high-
energy light that sinter metal particles instantaneously, and 
the temperature of the substrate remains low.  The effect of 
the different photonic sinteri ng profiles on the mechanical 
and electrical properties of the printed traces has been 
studied.  Electrical and mechan ical performance has been 
studied through the characterization of the frequency 
performance of low-pass filters, high-pass filters, and 
amplifiers fabricated usin g surface mount components on 
additively printed metallization.  Reliability and the 
performance degradation of the additively printed circuits 
have been quantified in flex-to-install applications.  In 
addition, SEM/EDAX has been used to study the 
intermetallics at the interface of LTS and additively printed 
circuits. 
Keywords: Direct-Write technique, Additive printing, 
Flexible electronics, Copper ink, Electrically Conductive 
Adhesive, Low-Temperature Solder, Reflow.   
INTRODUCTION 
Demand for the development of flexible-hybrid electronics 
manufacturing methods has been pushed by product 
developments that involve closer integration of electronics 
into the intended application.  The emergence of thin, flexible 
form-factors for electronics product design is a byproduct of 
the miniaturization of electr onics.  Traditional electronics 
frequently employ rigid printed circuit boards, which restricts 
the level of integration that may be achieved and the 
packaging required for dependable performance in the final 
application.  Through techniques for creating non-planar 
electronic designs, the development of additive print 
processes offers opportunities for closer integration of form 
and function.  Additionally, using additive techniques 
eliminates the mask and etch processes employed in stiff 
electronics manufacture using subtractive processing. 
Furthermore, additive printing allows for fewer production 
operations owing to fewer process steps and cost savings due 
to lower material usage [1], [2].  Less material consumption 
equals less environmental effect.  Lower temperature 
processing of printable materials makes it possible to employ 
flexible substrates like low-cost plastic films.   
Low-temperature films' viability requires using low-
temperature attachment methods for component assembly. 
Lower processing temperatures have other benefits, including 
lower warpage, lower energy consumption during 
manufacturing and asse mbly, and a lower carbon footprint. 
Low-temperature connectivity t echnologies for flat-panel 
displays, organic electronics, and low-cost disposable 
microelectronic devices on pl astic substrates are gaining 
popularity [3].  These substrates, such as PET, polyimide, and 
paper, as well as some electronic components based on 
organic groups or nanosized building blocks, are unable to 
maintain their functions and stabilities at temperatures ranging 
higher than 200C, which is required for melting conventional 
SnAgCu lead-free sold ers widely used in microelectronics 
soldering and reflow [4]-[6]. Thus, an enhanced 
interconnection method that allows for low-temperature 
processing while withstanding a greater operating temperature 
is desired.   
For a significant period of electronics development, the 
interconnection of components was dominated by the use of 
SnPb solders, which required higher processing temperatures 
greater than 200C.  The transition of electronics away from 
leaded solders resulted in th e emergence of SnAgCu-based 
solders, which also required higher temperature processing. 
More recently, several low-te mperature interconnection 
Proceedings of SMTA International, Oct 31 - Nov 3, 2022, Minneapolis, MN, USA.
15
As originally published in the SMTA Proceedings.
methods have emerged.  Electrically conductive adhesive 
(ECA) has emerged as a lead-free solder alternative in liquid 
crystal display (LCD), smart card, flip-chip assembly, chip-
scale package (CSP), and ball grid array (BGA) applications 
[7]-[8].  ECA is a class of materials comprised of fillers 
(whiskers, platelets, or particles) mixed in polymers (epoxy, 
silicone, or polyimide) that can provide binding strength and 
electrical conductivity between substrates and chips.  One of 
the primary benefits highlighted in ECAs is their low 
processing temperature compared  to conventional solders.  
Similarly, LTS paste processing allows for lower reflow 
temperatures, cutting energy usage during SMT by up to 40 
percent.  In order to satisfy the product objectives and 
maintain high system assembly yields at lower prices, 
compact and complex systems necessitate an evolution in both 
SMT methods and materials [9].   
This study focuses on performance interaction with 
different interconnection methods, including electrically 
conductive adhesive (ECA) and low-temperature solder 
(LTS).  Interconnection materials have been printed using the 
direct-write method to attach components such as resistors, 
inductors, capacitors, and LEDs to additively printed circuits.  
Components have been measured before and after attachment 
to examine interconnection pe rformance.  In addition, the 
cross-sectional area for the intermetallic formation was 
examined.  The Shear Test (Mechanical Property) of the 
attached components has also  been tested for various 
applications.  OrCAD software has been used to construct and 
evaluate an inverting Op-Amp circuit.  The performance of 
the inverting Op-Amp circuit has been compared for the 
interconnection materials used.   
 
PRINT-PLATFORM AND INKS 
For the printing, the direct-write technique was used on the 
nSrypt Printer.  Inks have been dispensed during the print 
process using Smart Pumps on the direct-write platform.  A 
Luer adapter nozzle has been used for the print process.  
Figure 1 shows the direct write setup used to print conductive 
and binding material.

A. Cu-Ink Description 
Test vehicle circuits were additively printed using a copper 
paste that can be processed post-print using laser and photonic 
sintering.  By adjusting the burst energies, photonic sintering 
enables the processing of the printed metallization at lower 
temperatures.  In addition, comp ared to silver inks, copper 
enables fabrication utilizing less expensive inks to construct 
circuits.  Prior to being loaded onto the direct-write print 
platform, the inks were blended in a planetary mixer.  Table I 
shows the ink properties of the conductive paste used in this 
paper.  Copper ink has a high solid concentration of 82 
percent and a viscosity of 25,000–30,000 cps.

The authors' earlier research with the ECA has determined 
that 150°C for 30 minutes is the ideal curing temperature for 
the silver-based ECA ink [10].  Conducive inks made of 
copper or silver can be used with the ECA.  Table II shows 
the ink properties of the ECA paste used in this paper.  The 
ECA used in the study is thixotropic with a viscosity of 
110,000 cP at a shear rate of 1 sec

C. Low-Temperature Solder (LTS) Paste LTS1 - SnBiAg 
LTS1 is a SnBiAg halogen-free, air-reflow capable, no-clean 
solder paste.  The paste has good wetting capabilities and is 
designed to leave a moderate residue.  The solder paste can be 
used in low-temperature connectivity with a lead-free solution 
thanks to its low-temperature activation.  The material 
composition is shown in Table III.  Progressive heating, 
followed by a cool-down profile, is used for processing.  A 
ramp rate of 0.5-1C/sec is used during the preheat portion of 
the reflow profile.  The melting point of the SnBiAg is 140C.   
 
Table III: SnBiAg LTS1 Ink properties 
Parameters Value 
Material Composition 57Bi/42Sn/1A g 
Heating Ramp Rate 0.5-1 ˚C/second 
Melting Point 140 ˚C 
D. Low-Temperature Solder (LTS) Paste LTS2 - SnIn 
LTS2 is a no-clean, halide-free SnIn solder paste.  Reduced 
metallization of precious metals like gold or silver 
leaching/scavenging and better ductility to compensate for 
thermal expansion coefficient (CTE) differences between 
different materials are two advantages of indium-containing 
alloys.  LTS2 is an air-reflow paste with a medium residue 
and good wetting properties.  The material composition is 
shown in Table IV.  A ramp rate of 0.5-1C/sec is used during 
the preheat portion of the reflow profile.  The melting point 
of the SnBiAg is 150C.

TEST VEHICLE DESIGN 
Test vehicles were developed and fabricated at the CAVE3 
Electronics Research Center.  Polyimide flexible substrate is 
used for printing processing and component assembly to 
fabricate the test vehicle.  The polyimide substrate is 5 mils 
thick and has been plasma-cl eaned at 100W for 5-minutes 
with oxygen.  The polyimide material can withstand 
temperatures of up to 300C.   
A. Test Vehicle Design – 1 
The test vehicle was created to investigate the sintering 
profile and print process.  Five lines represent each condition 
with pads at either end.  In order to guarantee that the printing 
is consistent for a certain process parameter, the number of 
traces is set to 5.  Each test condition is repeated three times 
to evaluate the variation in the print output.

Figure 2 shows this paper's test vehicle used for the 
sintering study.  For high print quality, Cu ink has been tested 
for different process factors, including printing speed and ink 
pressure.  Consistent ink deposition with a precise trace 
definition is a desirable print characteristic.  Mechanical and 
electrical characteristics have been investigated with respect 
to the changing process parame ters and their impact on the 
print profile including trace he ight and trace-width.  The 
mechanical and electrical properties have been assessed with 
the changes in the cross-section area.  Based on prior research 
by authors presented in [11], [13], the print process has been 
optimized to print at a print-speed of 3mm/s at 15psi ink-
pressure.  Voltage is used to define the maximum and peak 
energy for photonic cu ring.  The total energy is controlled 
through the specification of on-time and off-time in the burst 
sequence.  The photonic curing process can be run in several 
modes, including single-pulse, burst mode, which involves a 
specified duration pulse sequence, and continuous mode.  In 
addition, the curing profile can be controlled through the 
specification of the stage speed during pulse-flash, step-size, 
which defines the forward movement and flash energy 
overlap.  In the prior work, th e authors have also shown the 
influence of the sintering profile on the realized electrical and 
mechanical performance [13].  The effect of downstream 
thermal processes during component assembly on the prior 
realized print-properties is studied.   
B. Test Vehicle Design – 2 
The purpose of test design-2 is to establish the 
interconnection between discrete components and printed 
conductive traces to check the functionality of the ECA and 
LTS materials.  The proce ss-flow for attaching the 
components is shown in Figure 3.  Before being attached to 
the stage, the polyimide substrate used in this study was 
plasma cleaned.

LTS Paste 
This design is used to compar e measured values to rated

fiducial matching is followed by interconnection paste

printed pads on the interconnection material, followed by 
sinter or reflow in the oven for ECA and LTS, respectively.

REFLOW PROFILES FOR LTS 
The influence of sintering/reflow required for ECA 
processing and LTS processing has been studied to understand 
the effect of the downstream assembly steps on the prior 
realized performance of the additively printed circuits.  The 
ECA has been sintered at 150 C for 30-minutes.  Figure 7 
shows the reflow profile for LTS1 (SnBiAg).  A peak 
temperature of 25–45°C (175°C  shown) above the melting 
point of the solder alloy is needed to form a quality solder joint 
and achieve acceptable wetting and formation of intermetallic 
layer.  Figure 8 shows the reflow profile for LTS2 (SnIn).  A 
peak temperature of 25–45°C (215°C indicated) over the 
melting point of the solder alloy is required to establish a 
quality solder junction and ensure adequate wetting owing to 
the creation of an intermetallic layer.

EFFECT OF PHOTONIC SINTERING CONDITIONS 
In photonic sintering, several factors must be addressed 
including voltage, on-time, off-time, burst mode, stage speed 
and step size.  Each parameter modification defines the 
quantity of cure.  The system's aperture size is set at 80mm, 
the maximum slit size maintained throughout the study in this 
paper.  In this experimental leg, the photonic sintering process 
evolution with increased temp erature exposure due to the 
component attachment with varied sintering parameters is 
studied.   
 
Figure 10: Thermocouple numbering for temperature 
measurement during light flashing 
Thermocouples have been installed in the vicinity of the 
printed circuits to assess the temperature during the photonic 
sintering profile.  Figure 10 shows the positioning of 
thermocouples on the sample at each trace to measure the 
exposed temperature under different curing conditions.  The 
thermocouple labeled "1" is located beneath the polyimide 
substrate, while the remaining thermocouples are on the 
substrate's upper surface.  The temperature given in this 
section is the mean of the highest temperature readings 
observed.  The thermocouples continuously collect data 
throughout the flash. 
A. Effect of Pre-heat 
The effect of preheating th e traces after printing in a 
conventional oven for a brief time to dry up the ink before 
photonic sintering has been explored in order to avoid trace 
degradation.  Samples sintered without preheating 
experienced popcorn failure at various sintering parameters, 
as reported prior work by the authors[13].   
 
Figure 11: SEM of 
trace popcorn failure 
without preheating. 
 
Figure 12: EDX Analysis of one 
of the photonic cured samples 
without preheat 
The trace composition with and without preheat has been 
measured using EDX.  The samples were subsequently 
examined for the oxidation trace in both the with and without 
preheat samples.  The following profile was used to cure the 
samples for this investigation: (a) Voltage = 2000V (b) Energy 
= 1000 J (c) Frequency = 0.7 Hz (d) Burst Mode – 5 bursts.  
Figure 11 shows an SEM image of a sample without 
preheating at the most deteriorated pad location.  A circuit's 
degradation would result in circuit failure and unreliable 
operation.  Figure 12 shows an EDX analysis of a section of 
the area denoted by the red-bo xed region in Figure 11.  
Different locations have been identified, and their related 
element compositions have been reported using various color 
notations.  Even though the samples were photonically cured 
immediately after additive printing of copper, the traces 
oxidized on the surface, indi cating the necessity for an 
additional step for sintering copper.  The green and dark blue 
points extract data from the lowermost copper layer of the 
substrate, indicating a 100 percent copper element. 
Figure 13 depicts the sintering process, which includes a 
preheat step to aid in the evaporation of the solvent for proper 
photonic sintering of the printed conductive metal.  The 
approach also aids in reducing the chances of copper oxidation 
during sintering.

Figure 14 - Figure 16 shows the EDX analysis of printed 
copper traces at various pr eheat temperatures.  The 
information reported in the EDX research is based on an 
average of three samples.  It can be noticed that samples cured 
after preheating at 50°C and 60°C have no trace of oxygen 
element, but samples cured after preheating at 80°C have 
oxygen traces.  The popcorn type failure in the samples 
without preheating is due to the fast-transient heating in 
photonic sintering and the limited time available for the 
solvents to evaporate, resulting in deterioration.  Preheat is 
shown to be an effective strategy for avoiding popcorning 
failures in printed metallization. 
B. Effect of Flash Frequency 
The flash frequency plays an important role when 
sintering bigger-sized samples.  The parameters that were set 
Proceedings of SMTA International, Oct 31 - Nov 3, 2022, Minneapolis, MN, USA.
19
constant for this test are: (a) Voltage = 2000V (b) Energy = 
1000J (c) Stage motion: Continuous mode – 2mm/s.  The 
aperture size of the system is set to 80mm, which is the 
maximum mechanical aperture  spacing.  Increasing the 
frequency results in a shorter period, which allows the same 
amount of energy to be dissipated in a shorter amount of time.  
The quantity of cure should be more at a higher frequency 
since the energy flashes at a faster rate, which may help in the 
agglomeration of the conductive particles.  The lowest and 
maximum frequencies for the st ated total energy are 0.1 Hz 
and 0.7 Hz, respectively.

frequency 
Figure 17 shows the impact of varying frequency on the 
temperature dissipated on the substrate or trace during the 
sintering process.  The 0.7Hz frequency provides far better 
uniform sintering throughout the processed sample 
population.  Sample populaitons are thus subjected to similar 
temperatures, which is beneficial for bigger lot sizes or in a 
continuous industrial operation.

D. Effect of Varying Stage speed 
Sintering conditions should be studied using larger 
samples to be appropriate for mass manufacturing and larger 
circuit designs.  With varied speed and constant frequency, the 
proportion of overlap would change, affecting the amount of 
energy focused at a certain pl ace on the test vehicle.  With 
increasing speed, the overlap percentage decreases, so the 
degree of cure changes, making it critical to analyze this 
process.  Figure 20 directly compares the printed traces at 
different stage motions.  The resistance per unit length is 
consistently lower in the samples sintered at 1000J and ran at 
a stage speed of 2mm/s and a step size of 2mm under all 
preheat conditions.  Figure 21 illustrates a parallel comparison 
of the printed traces at different stage motions.  For all preheat 
parameters, the shear load to failure value of samples sintered 
at 750 J is greater than that of samples sintered at 1000J.  The 
samples sintered at 750J with a 60 C preheat run at a stage 
speed of 2mm/s and a step si ze of 2mm have the highest 
mechanical strength of all investigated conditions.  In order to 
examine the physics of the si ntering process and how it 
compares to standard sinterin g parameters, it was critical to 
determine the temperature at which the traces are exposed for 
each stage motion change.  For th is test, the parameters that 
were kept constant were as follows:: (a) Voltage = 2000V; (b) 
Energy = 1000J; (c) Frequency = 0.7 Hz.

Figure 22: Temperature of the surface vs Stage speed 
The temperature measured when the test vehicle is cured 
under defined conditions with different stage speeds and flash 
mode is depicted in Figure 22.  It was crucial to distinguish 
between continuous and burst modes.  As expected, the burst 
mode dissipated more heat at  the center trace, or where 
thermocouple number 4 is positioned.  The light's 
concentration was highest in the center and gradually dropped 
as it travelled away from it.  The flash intensity overlap varies 
in continuous mode depending on the frequency of the 
flashlight and the stage speed  combination, resulting in 
various sweep patterns.  When all conditions are considered, 
it is clear that the condition with a stage speed of 2mm/s and 
a frequency of 0.7 Hz has uniform temperature distribution 
across the test vehicle, providing further confirmation that the 
combination is the best for the set flashlight parameters for 
uniform sintering. 
E. Effect of Additional Temperature exposure due to 
interconnect sintering profile 
The component attachment procedure comprises extra 
sintering for the ECA or reflow for the solder, exposing the 
conducting material to more temperature exposure.  It is 
critical to examine the effect of this additional temperature 
exposure since over cure of  the traces may result in the 
formation of cracks or deterioration of the traces, resulting in 
poor mechanical and electri cal performance on component 
attachment.  Figure 23 depicts a comparison of the electrical 
performance of printed copper traces.  The results reflect a 
comparison of the electrical measurements of the traces with 
sintering conditions without the temperature profile for 
ECA/LTS indicated "before" and with additional temperature 
consideration designated as "after" for the conditions 
described previously.  The data is extremely important in 
determining the photonic sintering profile to utilize for various 
binding materials.  Samples for ECA, LTS1, and LTS2 would 
have the lowest resistance per unit length if sintered at 2mm/s 
at 750J flash energy.  1000J provides a stronger cure and 
might be utilized in regions with only printed traces and no 
components attachment or multilayer.  Figure 24 depicts a 
comparison of mechanical performance for printed traces 
before and after additional temperature conditions.  The 
optimal mechanical strength performance result changes 
depending on the condition and may be studied based on the 
final application.  Samples sintered at 2mm/s at 750J or 1000J 
are the average performance case scenario.

one of the resistors.  Understanding the mechanical strength 
of a component is significant because it may be utilized for 
failure analysis of a component in a flexible circuitry during 
bending, flexing, or twisting.  It is also critical to shear the 
components based on their overall height since this influences 
the shear strength of the component connection [12].  For the 
shear test the parameters include (a) Shear height = 20% of 
the component height from surface (b) Shear tip size 
=0.60mm (c) Test speed = 12.7 m/s.  Shear test results for 
various components are shown in Figure 30.  The shear 
strength of the components bonded using bonding material 
was calculated based on their heights.  The test height is 
determined by the component height, which eliminates the 
application of uneven shear fo rces.  According to the data 
shown above, the components with lower heights have higher 
mechanical strength.

II. SUMMARY
 AND CONCLUSIONS 
This paper studied various sintering conditions for 
additively printed copper on the direct-write platform.  Factors 
studied include preheat, stage speed, and flash energy.  The 
effect of preheating has been studied to assess the ability to 
mitigate the popcorning of printed metallization.  The EDX 
study was performed to demo nstrate that preheating is 
advantageous.  These test resu lts from test vehicle-1 were 
utilized to investigate the impact of additional curing 
processes in real-world applications.  Depending on the 
application, the test vehicl e's two design incorporates 
component attachments such as resistors, capacitors, 
inductors, and LEDs.  These are some of the most common 
circuitry components.  The direct-write approach for 
connecting the components has been established.  In order to 
estimate the percentage variation introduced by the additive 
printing attachment, the components were tested before and 
after the attachment.  The measured values were well within 
the rated values of the manufacturer.  For this study, the lack 
of understanding about the various binding materials was 
taken into account, and correct attachment issues were shown.  
Compared to silve r-based ECA and SnIn low-temperature 
solders, SnBiAg solder provid es the greatest performance.  
The EDX analysis is also used  to address the causes of the 
failure situations.  Based on the study of the test vehicle-1, the 
functionality of the component attachment was further 
investigated by building an amplified inverting Op-Amp 
circuitry.  The design was initially modeled in software and 
compared to the system's real performance.  Although a 15V 
DC source supplied the circuit, no degradation in the printed 
traces was noticed.  This also means that the technology may 
be used for high-power circuits like this one.  The 
performance comparison produces favorable results, 
indicating that the process development was carried out in 
order to develop functioning circuitry.  All three binding 
materials give a fundamental kno wledge of the material in 
high-frequency applications.
